name: Algorithm Design Fundamentals
tagline: Complexity (O, Omega, Theta), Master Theorem for divide-and-conquer, sorting (insertion, merge, quick), search (binary, exponential, 2D-grid), and dynamic programming (Fibonacci, Kadane). Picking the right algorithmic pattern is half the battle.

blocks.text: Asymptotic notation: f = O(g) means f <= c*g eventually (upper bound); f = Omega(g) means f >= c*g (lower bound); f = Theta(g) means both. The Master Theorem solves T(n) = a*T(n/b) + f(n) by comparing f(n) to n^(log_b a): the larger one wins. Examples: merge sort (a=b=2, f(n)=n) gives Theta(n log n); binary search (a=1, b=2, f=1) gives Theta(log n).

Sorting: insertion (Theta(n^2) worst, fast on small/nearly-sorted); merge (Theta(n log n), stable, O(n) extra space); quick (Theta(n log n) average, Theta(n^2) worst with bad pivot, in-place). Quick is usually fastest in practice due to cache efficiency. Search: binary (Theta(log n) for sorted), exponential search + binary search (Theta(log n) for unknown-length sorted arrays), top-right corner pointer (Theta(n) for n x n sorted 2D grid).

Dynamic programming kills exponential blow-up by memoizing overlapping subproblems. Naive recursive Fibonacci is O(phi^n) ~ O(1.618^n); iterative DP is O(n); matrix exponentiation is O(log n). For the maximum-subarray problem, Kadane's idea of tracking T_min as a running prefix-sum minimum reduces the naive O(n^2) sweep to O(n).

Randomized algorithms: Knuth shuffle gives a uniformly random permutation in Theta(n) (vs Theta(n log n) for sort-by-random-key). Reservoir sampling lets you uniformly sample from a stream of unknown length using O(1) memory. Both rely on a clean inductive invariant: after processing item i, every prior item has equal probability of being kept.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Algorithm questions reward pattern recognition: 'sorted input' -> binary search; 'rolling window' -> sliding sum; 'overlapping subproblems' -> DP; 'unknown length' -> doubling / reservoir sampling; 'count comparisons' -> pairing trick. Always state the recurrence first, then apply Master Theorem; always state the loop invariant first, then prove correctness.

whenToUse: Estimating runtime / space for a candidate algorithm before coding it.
Choosing among sort variants based on input characteristics.
Designing a data-stream algorithm with bounded memory.
Reducing exponential recursion to polynomial via memoization or matrix forms.
Bit-level tricks for hot loops (bit-counting, isPowerOf2).
recipe: Write the recurrence T(n) = a*T(n/b) + f(n); apply Master Theorem.
For sliding-window sums, maintain a running total: subtract the leaving element, add the entering one.
For sorted-array search, use binary search; if length unknown, double-then-binary-search (Theta(log n)).
For shuffle, use Knuth: for i=0..n-1, swap A[i] with A[Random(i, n-1)].
For max contiguous subarray, sweep prefix sums and track T_min.
commonPitfalls: Confusing average-case with worst-case; quicksort is Theta(n log n) on average but Theta(n^2) on a sorted input with first-element pivot.
Off-by-one in binary search bounds (lo, hi inclusive vs exclusive).
Generating a 'random shuffle' via Random(0, n-1) at each step -- this gives n^n outcomes, not n!, so distribution is biased.
Using sort-by-random-key when keys can collide -- then ties create non-uniform permutations.
Allocating a fresh O(n) array each merge step in merge sort (write merge in-place into a single buffer instead).

appearsIn.title: Find Min and Max with 3n/2 Comparisons
